researchNotes: Unusually rich real evidence for the South Indian History half of this clause — 5 confirmed hits across 2017/2022/2025/2026, including a classic 'swapped pairs' trap (Chola and Pandya capitals deliberately exchanged in the wrong options). The Vijayanagar/Bahmani half had no findable direct past-paper hit, so it rests on standard, well-established history reference facts. IMPORTANT CORRECTION (2026-07 pass): while re-verifying the Q158/r02/m01 'Chola Queen Vanamadevi Sati' fact by opening the actual 2025_q1 page 93 exam image (not just OCR text), discovered the original entry had the WRONG plate — the checkmark is clearly on option (B) Tiruvalangadu Plate, not (A) Anbil Plate as previously recorded. Fixed r02 and m01 to the correct answer; this is exactly the kind of factual error the 'verify before touching' append-only rule exists to catch. Also closed real gaps in the Vijayanagar/Bahmani half: added Hampi as the empire's capital (previously never named at all despite being the single most iconic fact about Vijayanagar), the Varaha gold coin, the Battle of Talikota (1565) and its consequences, Bahmani PM Mahmud Gawan, the five Deccan Sultanates that succeeded the Bahmani Kingdom, and the Portuguese travellers (Domingo Paes, Fernão Nuniz) who documented the Vijayanagara court — all standard, well-established facts cross-checked across multiple reference sites. A coaching-aggregator claim characterizing two Bahmani sultans as 'peace-loving' vs 'cruel and merciless' could not be independently confirmed (Wikipedia/ClearIAS complicate that framing) and was deliberately excluded rather than risk shipping a shaky claim.

reading.intro: Third topic of Unit III, combining medieval Deccan kingdoms with ancient/medieval South Indian (Sangam-age and Chola) history. Real exam evidence shows a strong South-Indian-history lean with a classic trap format: swapping two kingdoms' correct facts between each other as wrong-answer bait.

qa.question: Real exam (trap format): Match each king/dynasty to its correct capital pair — Chera King, Chola King, Pandiya King, King Ashoka. Which pairings are correct: Chera-Vanji/Thondi, Chola-Madurai/Korkai, Pandiya-Uraiyur/Poombugar, Ashoka-Samba Inscription?
qa.answer: Only Chera-Vanji/Thondi and Ashoka-Samba Inscription are correct. The Chola and Pandya pairings are SWAPPED in the trap: actually, Chola's capitals were Uraiyur and Poompuhar (not Madurai-Korkai), and the Pandya capitals were Madurai and Korkai (not Uraiyur-Poombugar). Always double check which Tamil dynasty owned which historic capital pair — this exact swap is a recurring trap.

qa.question: Real exam: Which inscription/plate exposed that the Chola Queen Vanamadevi committed Sati?
qa.answer: The Tiruvalangadu Plate (image-verified answer key; a previous version of this note incorrectly said Anbil Plate — corrected 2026-07 pass after re-checking the actual exam page image, where option (B) Tiruvalangadu Plate carries the checkmark, not (A) Anbil Plate).

qa.question: Real exam: During the Later Chola period, what was 'Irayili'?
qa.answer: Tax-free land (not taxed land, temple-leviable tax, or leasing-land tax).

qa.question: Real exam: What was the Chola kingdom's revenue department called?
qa.answer: Puravuvari (not Perumtharam, Utan Kuttam, or Olai Nayagam).

qa.question: Real exam: Arrange these Sangam-age Chera kings chronologically — Senkuttuvan, Emayavaramban, Kanaikal Irumporai, Kuttuvan Cheral.
qa.answer: Emayavaramban → Senkuttuvan → Kuttuvan Cheral → Kanaikal Irumporai.

qa.question: When was the Vijayanagar Empire founded, by whom, and what dynasties ruled it?
qa.answer: Founded in 1336 by brothers Harihara I and Bukka Raya I, of the Sangama dynasty. Four dynasties ruled it over time: Sangama, Saluva, Tuluva, and Aravidu.

qa.question: Who was Krishnadevaraya, and what were his key achievements?
qa.answer: The greatest Vijayanagar king, of the Tuluva dynasty, ruled 1509-1529. He extended the empire across most of South India, defeated the Bahmani Sultans and Portuguese, captured Bahmani forts (Bidar, Gulbarga, Raichur, Bijapur), made major temple donations (adding towering gopuras), and authored the Telugu poem Amuktamalyada (the story of Andal).

qa.question: Who founded the Bahmani Kingdom?
qa.answer: Alauddin Hasan, an Afghan officer under Muhammad bin Tughlaq, who took the title Alauddin Hasan Bahman Shah.

qa.question: What was the long-running cause of conflict between Vijayanagar and the Bahmani Kingdom?
qa.answer: A dispute over the Raichur Doab — the fertile region between the Krishna and Tungabhadra rivers (also extending to disputes over the Krishna-Godavari delta).

qa.question: What is the modern name of the ruins of the Vijayanagar Empire's capital city, and where is it located?
qa.answer: Hampi, on the banks of the Tungabhadra river in present-day Karnataka — a UNESCO World Heritage Site today, and the single most iconic surviving symbol of the empire.

qa.question: What were the Vijayanagara Empire's gold coins called?
qa.answer: Varaha (also known in some sources as 'Pagoda' or 'Hun') — named after the Varaha (boar) incarnation of Vishnu depicted on them.

qa.question: When was the Battle of Talikota fought, who fought it, and what was the outcome?
qa.answer: 23 January 1565 — a combined alliance of the Deccan Sultanates (Bijapur, Golconda, Ahmadnagar, and Bidar) defeated the Vijayanagara army under regent Rama Raya. The defeat led to the sack and destruction of the capital, Hampi, and marked the beginning of Vijayanagara's decline.

qa.question: Who was Mahmud Gawan, and what was his contribution to the Bahmani Kingdom?
qa.answer: A Persian-origin statesman who rose to become the Bahmani Kingdom's chief minister (from 1458) and de facto ruler as Prime Minister (1466-1481). Under his administration the Bahmani Sultanate reached its greatest territorial extent; he is also remembered for founding the Mahmud Gawan Madrasa at Bidar, a major centre of religious and secular education.

qa.question: After the Bahmani Kingdom broke up (around 1527), which five Deccan Sultanates succeeded it?
qa.answer: Bijapur (Adil Shahi dynasty — famous for the Gol Gumbaz and its whispering gallery), Golconda (Qutb Shahi dynasty), Ahmadnagar (Nizam Shahi dynasty), Bidar (Barid Shahi dynasty), and Berar (Imad Shahi dynasty). Four of these five (all but Berar) later formed the alliance that defeated Vijayanagar at Talikota.

qa.question: Which Portuguese travellers visited and documented the Vijayanagara Empire's court and capital city, giving historians valuable eyewitness accounts?
qa.answer: Domingo Paes and Fernão Nuniz — their detailed travel accounts (describing Krishnadevaraya's court, Hampi's markets, and courtly ceremonies) are among the most important non-Indian primary sources for Vijayanagara history.

questions.prompt: Which inscription/plate exposed that the Chola Queen Vanamadevi committed Sati?
questions.explanation: Tiruvalangadu Plate (image-verified against the answer key — corrected 2026-07 pass from a previous, wrong 'Anbil Plate' entry).

questions.prompt: Which dynasty's historic capitals were Madurai and Korkai?
questions.explanation: Pandya — a common trap swaps this with Chola.

questions.prompt: Which dynasty's historic capitals were Uraiyur and Poompuhar?
questions.explanation: Chola — a common trap swaps this with Pandya.

questions.prompt: During the Later Chola period, 'Irayili' referred to:
The Chola kingdom's revenue department was called:
The Vijayanagar Empire was founded in 1336 by which brothers?
options: Harihara I and Bukka Raya I
questions.explanation: Harihara I and Bukka Raya I, of the Sangama dynasty.

questions.prompt: Krishnadevaraya, the greatest Vijayanagar king, belonged to which dynasty?
The long-running Vijayanagar-Bahmani conflict was mainly over control of:
questions.explanation: The Raichur Doab, between the Krishna and Tungabhadra rivers.

questions.prompt: The ruins of the Vijayanagara Empire's capital city survive today at which UNESCO World Heritage Site, on the Tungabhadra river?
The Vijayanagara Empire's gold coins, named after an incarnation of Vishnu, were called:
The Battle of Talikota (1565), which led to the sack of Hampi, was fought between Vijayanagara and:
questions.explanation: A confederacy of Deccan Sultanates (Bijapur, Golconda, Ahmadnagar, and Bidar) defeated Vijayanagara's regent Rama Raya.

questions.prompt: Mahmud Gawan, who expanded the Bahmani Kingdom to its greatest territorial extent as Prime Minister (1466-1481), also founded a famous madrasa at:
Which Deccan Sultanate, ruled by the Qutb Shahi dynasty, emerged after the Bahmani Kingdom's breakup?
questions.explanation: Golconda (Qutb Shahi dynasty); Bijapur was ruled by the Adil Shahis, Ahmadnagar by the Nizam Shahis.

questions.prompt: Which two Portuguese travellers left detailed eyewitness accounts of Krishnadevaraya's court and the city of Hampi?
Krishnadevaraya, the greatest Vijayanagar king, authored which Telugu literary work?
The Gol Gumbaz, famous for its whispering gallery, was built at Bijapur under which Bahmani-successor dynasty?
